merger_name: Coles – land around supermarket site in Balaclava, VIC
merger_description:
Coles Group Limited (**Coles**) is an Australian public company (ASX: COL) that primarily operates:

* supermarkets nationwide through its subsidiary, Coles Supermarkets Australia Pty Ltd, and
* retail liquor stores nationwide, trading under the Liquorland, Liquorland Warehouse and Liquorland Cellars brands.

Coles also has a property development arm, Coles Group Property Developments Ltd (**CGPD**), that buys land and constructs shopping centres, standalone supermarkets and/or liquor outlets and then sells these completed sites to third party buyers.

The Port Phillip City Council (**Council**) is the local government authority overseeing the City of Port Phillip, an area in inner Melbourne including the suburbs of Albert Park, Balaclava, Elwood, part of the Melbourne CBD, Middle Park, part of Port Melbourne, Ripponlea, South Melbourne, Southbank, St Kilda, St Kilda East, St Kilda West and Windsor.

Coles currently operates a full line supermarket and a Liquorland store at the intersection of Alfred Street and Camden Street, Balaclava VIC 3183. Coles owns the land on which these stores are situated, along with several adjoining parcels (including land leased to other retailers and sections of the carpark behind the supermarket).

Balaclava is a suburb within the City of Port Phillip local government area in Victoria, situated approximately 8 kilometres from Melbourne’s CBD.

The Council has offered to sell Coles a number of adjacent Council-owned parcels of land located at Nelson Street and Alfred Street to facilitate a significant revitalisation of the Balaclava precinct. This will involve the development of a new mixed-use retail and residential precinct. CGPD intends to enter into an unconditional contract of sale with the Council (the **Acquisition**).

The land proposed for sale includes part of the carpark adjacent to the Coles supermarket area enclosed by Carlisle, Alfred, Nelson, and Camden Streets, and some laneways that will be discontinued, which is approximately 2,240 sqm of land in total.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Coles Group Property Developments Limited (CGPD), a property
development arm of Coles Group Limited (Coles), applied for a
notification waiver in respect of CGPD’s proposed acquisition of
parcels of land located at Nelson Street and Alfred Street, adjacent
to the Coles supermarket in Balaclava, Victoria, from the City of Port
Phillip, as described in the transaction documents provided as part
of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

Coles is an Australian public company (ASX: COL) that primarily
operates:
• supermarkets nationwide, and
• retail liquor stores nationwide, trading under the Liquorland,
Liquorland Warehouse and Liquorland Cellars brands.
Coles has a property development arm, CGPD, that buys land and
constructs shopping centres, standalone supermarkets and/or liquor
outlets and then sells these completed sites to third party buyers.
The vendor, the Port Phillip City Council, is the local government
authority overseeing the City of Port Phillip, an area in inner
Melbourne including the suburbs of Albert Park, Balaclava, Elwood,
part of the Melbourne CBD, Middle Park, part of Port Melbourne,
Ripponlea, South Melbourne, Southbank, St Kilda, St Kilda East,
St Kilda West and Windsor.
The target land proposed for sale includes part of the carpark
adjacent to the Coles supermarket area enclosed by Carlisle, Alfred,
Nelson, and Camden Streets, and some laneways that will be
discontinued.
The freehold interest would be an input to Coles’ supermarket
retailing operations.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided by the applicant and had regard to the
factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and Consumer Act
2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. Coles currently owns the land on which its supermarket in
Balaclava is located and the adjacent land that is the subject
of the Acquisition is unlikely to be able to accommodate a
new supermarket
b. the land that is proposed to be acquired will be used to
improve the quality of the offering of Coles’ existing
supermarket in Balaclava
c. there are several alternative grocery retailers, including
Woolworths, ALDI and IGA, and retail suppliers of packaged
liquor for offsite consumption, in and around the local area.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds determined under section 51ABP(1) being met. The ACCC
does however observe that the targeted notification requirements for
supermarkets determined under section 51ABQ(1) are likely to apply.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.
